ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pork chop spaghetti; FOODON_00002572 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ (Pasta dishes) is the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for pork chop spaghetti.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pork chop spaghetti as served in a Singapore hawker centre or Western food stall, comprising approximately 200 g cooked spaghetti with tomato sauce and one pan-fried pork chop (~150 g), totalling approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Chop Spaghetti — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Pork Chop Spaghetti (cr_id F05060099). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked spaghetti with meat sauce (NDB 27116) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this composite dish; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 49 applied on the basis of the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white spaghetti (boiled, mean GI ~46–49), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The composite dish GI is estimated as low (49) reflecting the spaghetti base modulated by protein and fat from the pork chop and tomato sauce. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (49 × 9.82 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 16.8 for a 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 312 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,092 mg sodium, representing ~48% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, use low-sodium tomato sauce, and limit portion size to 200–250 g to keep per-meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 9.82 g/100 g with low dietary fibre (0.88 g/100 g); a 350 g serving provides ~34 g available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~17, which is moderate and requires portion control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200 g, substitute white spaghetti with wholemeal or legume-based pasta to increase fibre and reduce postprandial glucose excursion, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 4.4 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~1.4 g/100 g; sodium 312 mg/100 g; pork chop contributes dietary cholesterol and saturated fat relevant to LDL management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose a lean pork loin cut over a fatty chop, use olive oil sparingly, opt for a low-sodium tomato sauce, and limit serving size to 250 g to manage saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) which can amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; tomato sauce is a nightshade-derived ingredient that may trigger sensitivity in some RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting pork chop with grilled salmon or chicken breast to reduce arachidonic acid load, and monitor personal response to nightshade-containing tomato sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Chop Spaghetti is a popular Western-style dish commonly found in Singapore hawker centres and Western food stalls. It consists of a pan-fried or grilled pork chop served atop or alongside boiled white spaghetti coated in a tomato-based sauce, often seasoned with garlic, onion, and herbs. The dish reflects Singapore's adaptation of Western cuisine into an affordable, accessible hawker format. The pork chop is typically tenderised, marinated, and pan-fried until golden, yielding a slightly crispy exterior with a juicy interior. The tomato sauce provides mild acidity and sweetness, complementing the savoury pork. Per 100 g, the dish provides 113 kcal, 8.44 g protein, 4.4 g fat, 9.82 g carbohydrate, 0.88 g dietary fibre, and 312 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 49 (low), though the low fibre content provides limited glycaemic buffering. Clinically, the sodium content is a concern for hypertension at larger serving sizes, and the refined carbohydrate base warrants portion control for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets due to pork content. It is a common working-lunch option in Singapore and is typically served as a single-plate meal.
common_user_queries: Is pork chop spaghetti suitable for diabetics?
猪排意大利面 营养

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060099) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous entry and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database spaghetti reference and documented as analogous estimate. All mandatory multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite Singapore hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F05060099, and no sufficiently close composite match was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for spaghetti with meat sauce (NDB 27116), adjusted proportionally for the pork chop component; this is documented as an adapted value. GI of 49 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white spaghetti; the composite dish GI is modulated downward by protein and fat from the pork chop. Glycemic load per serving (16.8) calculated for a 350 g standard plate: (49 × 9.82 × 3.5) / 100 = 16.8. The dish is not halal due to pork content. Dietary tag 'low_calorie' from input is retained as contextual metadata only; at 113 kcal/100 g and a 350 g serving (~396 kcal), the dish is moderate in caloric density rather than strictly low-calorie.
